Journal.Title: Annual International Conference of the IEEE Engineering in Medicine and Biology Society. IEEE Engineering in Medicine and Biology Society. Annual International Conference
Journal.ISOAbbreviation: Annu Int Conf IEEE Eng Med Biol Soc

Article.ArticleTitle: Assessing the Sensitivity and Localization Accuracy of Electromagnetic Source Imaging via Computational Simulation with Realistic Data.

Abstract.AbstractText: Children with drug resistant epilepsy (DRE) require neurosurgery to achieve seizure freedom. Successful neurosurgery depends on precisely delineating the epileptogenic zone (EZ), often approximated by epileptic interictal biomarkers such as spikes. Spikes can be recorded noninvasively using high-density EEG (HD-EEG) and magnetoencephalography (MEG), with their generators localized via electric and magnetic source imaging (ESI/MSI). Prior EEG and MEG studies have shown that ~30% of spikes can be seen in only one modality possibly due to sensitivity differences. Here, we examine the sensitivity of HD-EEG and MEG, and localization accuracy of ESI, MSI, and combined electromagnetic source imaging (EMSI) using realistic simulated data generated from patient's intracranial EEG data. We modeled dipolar sources of various orientations at specific epilepsy-related brain regions in eight children with DRE. For each source, we generated realistic HD-EEG and MEG data with spikes. We investigated the relation between source configurations (i.e., location and orientation) and spike detectability with EEG and MEG. For ESI, MSI, and EMSI we assessed the relation of sensitivity and localization accuracy for all source configurations and regions. We observed that the spike detectability and signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) of HD-EEG and MEG depend on source orientation: EEG was more sensitive to radial sources (at gyral crest and sulcal bottom), while MEG favored tangential sources (at sulci walls). MEG showed higher localization accuracy (3.39±5.21 mm) for tangential sources compared to HD-EEG (9.02±13.09 mm). For sources located at the sulci walls, EMSI (2.02±3.95 mm) outperformed both ESI (9.02±13.09 mm) and MSI (3.39±5.21 mm). Our findings support the notion that EEG and MEG offer complementary information when epileptiform activity is visible in only one modality, while EMSI has additive value in the presurgical evaluation of children with DRE compared to individual modalities.Clinical Relevance - Assessing the sensitivity profiles of EEG and MEG as well as the localization accuracy of ESI, MSI, and EMSI for various configurations of epileptogenic sources would allow a better understanding of the abilities and limitations of these modalities in the noninvasive characterization of the irritative zone in presurgical evaluation of patients with DRE.

MedlineJournalInfo.MedlineTA: Annu Int Conf IEEE Eng Med Biol Soc
